intro: Every default value in NekoServe comes from somewhere. Below are all the cited works with DOIs, APA formatting, and BibTeX entries. When writing the thesis, just grab the whole block.
downloadAllBibtexHint: Download every citation as a single BibTeX file. Paste into your LaTeX project's bib directory and cite via \cite{hirsch2025cats} etc.
noParamLink: Not referenced by any parameter (supporting literature only)

hero.title: How 8 papers hold my cat-café simulator together
hero.lede: This page walks through every cited work and the part of the simulator it powers, as one story. We start with Little's 1961 queueing identity, move through Ancker & Gafarian's 1963 balking/reneging model, and land on Hirsch et al.'s 2025 ethological study where they watched 27 cats in a Swedish cat café for 227 hours. Scroll down to read it chapter by chapter.

landscape.ariaLabel: Three-tier citation landscape: classical theory, service empirics, cat-café behavior
landscape.ariaLabelMethodology: Three-tier methodology landscape: simulation V&V, distance metrics, statistical inference
landscape.hoverHint: Hover to dim the other tiers; click a card for a short summary of the paper.

little1961proof.kind: 1961 foundational queueing-theory proof in Operations Research
little1961proof.contribution: Proved L = λ·W: average number in the system = arrival rate × average time in system, independent of service-time distribution. This is the math behind every "average queue length" / "average in-system count" statistic in my simulator.

ancker1963balking1.contribution: First paper to formally incorporate balking (refusing to join a visible queue) into M/M/1 analysis. In my simulator this maps to customers leaving when the queue appears too long.

ancker1963balking2.kind: 1963 follow-up from the same authors in Operations Research
ancker1963balking2.contribution: Extended the prior paper to include reneging (abandoning the queue after waiting). I cite this paper as the theoretical basis for my maxWaitTime mechanism.

hasugian2020analysis.kind: 2020 empirical service-industry study in IOP Conference Series
hasugian2020analysis.contribution: Field-measured customer arrivals at an Indonesian fast-food restaurant and confirmed exponential inter-arrivals / Poisson totals. My customerArrivalInterval (Exp(λ) sampling) cites this paper.

dbeis2024enhancing.kind: 2024 M/M/1 extension in Journal of Management Analytics
dbeis2024enhancing.contribution: Measured reneging behavior in a drive-thru and folded it into the ρ formula (ρ_R = (λ - RR) / μ). The ρ_R correction shown on my How-It-Works page cites this paper directly.

li2025attributes.contribution: Surveyed pet-café satisfaction sources and decomposed them into environment / price / service attributes. Informs the multi-dimensional view behind my customerSatisfactionScore design.

hirsch2025cats.kind: 2025 core behavioral-ethology field study in Animals
hirsch2025cats.contribution: 227 hours of continuous observation of 27 cats in a Swedish cat café, producing a 9-state ethogram, vertical-level distribution, and a 4-customer-type × 4-interaction-mode matrix. This is the empirical backbone of the whole NekoServe simulator.

ropski2023analysis.kind: 2023 animal-welfare study in Journal of Veterinary Behavior
ropski2023analysis.contribution: Compared cat health and length-of-stay between cat cafés and foster-based rescue. A key reminder that cats are stakeholders, not stage dressing. My CatWelfareScore extends this perspective.

sargent2013vv.kind: 2013 methodology classic in Journal of Simulation on simulation V&V
sargent2013vv.contribution: Lays out the full verification & validation workflow (conceptual / data / operational validity). My Validation page is structured after this framework: check inputs first, then compare outputs against empirical benchmarks.

kleijnen1995vv.kind: 1995 foundational V&V paper in European Journal of Operational Research
kleijnen1995vv.contribution: Decomposes simulation validation into face validity, historical-data comparison, and sensitivity analysis. My sensitivity sweeps and Hirsch-baseline comparisons trace back to this layering.

rubner2000emd.kind: 2000 distance-metric paper in International Journal of Computer Vision
rubner2000emd.contribution: Earth Mover's Distance (EMD) gives a principled way to measure how close two distributions are. The Validation page compares NekoServe's ethogram against Hirsch's 9-state distribution with this metric.

agresti2013cda.kind: 2013 Wiley textbook on categorical data analysis
agresti2013cda.contribution: Provides the inference toolkit for ethogram-style categorical distributions: chi-square tests, proportion CIs, and multinomial comparisons. All goodness-of-fit tests on the Validation page follow this playbook.

wilson1927ci.kind: 1927 JASA paper introducing the Wilson score interval
wilson1927ci.contribution: Wilson's score interval is a small-sample-robust way to bound a proportion p, better than the normal approximation. I use it for error bars on balking rates, reneging rates, and other percentage metrics.

radial.ariaLabel: Parameter-source radial map: NekoServe's 15 cited parameters grouped by source paper
radial.hoverHint: Hover a paper node to highlight every parameter it backs; click a parameter chip to see its default value and source note.

benchmark.title: Hirsch 2025 · 9-state ethogram validation benchmark
benchmark.ariaLabel: 9-state ethogram benchmark bar chart with Wilson 95% confidence intervals
benchmark.hoverHint: Hover any row for exact proportion, sample count, and 95% CI; click to pin a full description of that state.
benchmark.formulaIntro: The black whisker in the chart is a Wilson CI. Compared to the normal-approximation interval, Wilson stays well-behaved when p is near 0 or 1 and when n is small, which is why even a 0.3% PLAYING slice still gets reasonable bounds. The formula comes from the Wilson CI methodology paper.

pWilson.label: Wilson-corrected proportion
pWilson.desc: Nudges the raw p̂ toward 0.5 before forming the interval, so the CI does not blow up when p is near 0 or 1. The center of the whisker in the chart is this value, not p̂ itself.
pWilson.example: For HIDDEN, p̂ = 10.7% and the Wilson-corrected center is ~10.8%, CI = [10.1%, 11.3%].

pHat.label: Sample proportion
pHat.desc: The raw empirical fraction: count of that state divided by total scans. This is the number Hirsch reports directly in Figure 3.
pHat.example: Here p̂ = 12.8% means SOCIALIZING occurred 1,601 times out of 12,505 scans.

n.label: Total samples
n.desc: Sample size across the ethogram dataset. Larger n shrinks the Wilson CI; for small n the Wilson interval is much more robust than the normal approximation.
n.example: Hirsch's 227 observation hours accumulate n = 12,505 scans.

z.label: Standard normal quantile
z.desc: Controls the confidence level. 90% / 95% / 99% use different z; higher confidence = larger z = wider interval.

shrinkage.label: Wilson shrinkage term
shrinkage.desc: The correction that pulls the estimate toward 0.5. As n grows this term vanishes and Wilson collapses back to the normal approximation; when n is small it rescues edge proportions from exploding.
shrinkage.example: For PLAYING (p̂ = 0.3%, n = 12,505) the shrinkage is ~0.00015 — invisible. With n = 50 it would pull 0.3% up to ~2.1%.

halfWidth.label: Interval half-width
halfWidth.desc: The ± portion is the Wilson half-width — the uncertainty bound. The whisker ticks in the chart sit at center ± this value.
halfWidth.example: For OUT_OF_LOUNGE (p̂ = 31.6%, n = 12,505), the half-width is ~±0.8pp, so the whisker is short.

description.OUT_OF_LOUNGE: The cat is not in the observable lounge — typically retreated to staff space or a cat-only room. Hirsch breaks this out to stress that cats retain an exit option.
description.RESTING: In the lounge but relaxed, still, low-arousal (lying, curled, light sleep). The most common cat state inside cat cafés.
description.SOCIALIZING: Close-range interaction with a human or another cat: contact, sniffing, being petted, co-sleeping.
description.HIDDEN: Tucked under furniture, inside boxes, or in high corners. Different from OUT_OF_LOUNGE — the cat is still in-space but out of sight.
description.ALERT: Awake and sitting/standing, watching a direction, not moving or interacting. A neutral vigilant state.
description.GROOMING: Licking or tidying fur. Short, steady, a welfare indicator of comfort.
description.MOVING: Walking, jumping, relocating. The scan caught the cat mid-motion.
description.EXPLORING: Investigating novel objects or toys. More active than MOVING — directed curiosity.
description.PLAYING: Pouncing, batting, chasing with clear play intent. Hirsch measured this at only 0.3%, the lowest of all states.

nekoUse.OUT_OF_LOUNGE: Cats can leave the viewable area (off-duty). In playback, that ~31.6% naturally drops off-screen.
nekoUse.RESTING: The cat FSM sets RESTING as the highest-weight idle state, calibrated to the 31.7% benchmark.
nekoUse.SOCIALIZING: Drives customer–cat interaction events; catInteractionTime sampling lands SOCIALIZING near 12.8%.
nekoUse.HIDDEN: Hidden removes the cat from the interaction pool — maps to the welfare-stress signal described in Ropski 2023.
nekoUse.ALERT: Alert cats don't initiate customer approaches; in-sim it acts as a gating precondition for SOCIALIZING.
nekoUse.GROOMING: Counts as a positive-weight term in CatWelfareScore (comfort sub-indicator).
nekoUse.MOVING: Drives the cat-walking animation frames during playback.
nekoUse.EXPLORING: Boosting exploration visibility raises CatWelfareScore, aligned with Hirsch's environmental-enrichment discussion.
nekoUse.PLAYING: The 'most ideal' welfare indicator; scenario comparisons surface how PLAYING share shifts under different conditions.

ch1a.title: Little's Law: the root of every queue statistic
ch1a.narrative: Little (1961) proved a deceptively simple identity: the average number in the system L, the arrival rate λ, and the average time-in-system W always satisfy L = λ·W, no matter the service distribution. Every "average queue length" / "average in-system count" statistic in my simulator is an extension of this equation. The gauge on the right shows λ and W wiggling together with L tracking them live. Hit play to see λ ramp from 0.5 to 2.0 and watch L grow linearly.

ch1b.title: Balking vs reneging: two flavors of giving up
ch1b.narrative: Ancker & Gafarian's two 1963 papers form one complete abandonment-behavior model. Balking is a customer who sees the queue at the door and turns around immediately ("I'm not queuing for that"). Reneging is someone who already joined but can't wait any longer and bails mid-queue ("forget it, next time"). My simulator encodes these as two distinct triggers: balking fires off a queue-length threshold, reneging fires off accumulated wait > maxWaitTime. The animation plays both side by side.

ch2a.title: Hasugian 2020: customer arrivals ≈ Poisson
ch2a.narrative: Hasugian et al. (2020) measured real arrivals at an Indonesian fast-food restaurant and found inter-arrival times were exponential and total counts were Poisson-shaped. That justifies my customerArrivalInterval parameter: I sample gaps from Exp(λ) and let customers walk in one at a time, reproducing the bursty-then-quiet flow this paper measured. The scripted scene compresses 30 arrivals into 5 seconds so you can clearly see the bursty / bunchy nature of Poisson arrivals.

ch2b.title: Dbeis & Al-Sahili 2024: reneging has a time distribution
ch2b.narrative: Dbeis & Al-Sahili (2024) measured in a drive-thru how long each customer waited before giving up, and re-derived the classical ρ formula to account for reneging (see Ch. 1.5's ρ_R correction). My simulator approximates their distribution with a hard maxWaitTime threshold: wait past it and you're out. The scripted scene renders a single customer's patience as a red arc; when it drains, they fade away, matching the paper's "past the tolerance, leave" semantic.

ch2c.title: Li 2025: pet-café satisfaction has three attribute dimensions
ch2c.narrative: Li et al. (2025) surveyed pet-café patrons and teased out three dominant satisfaction attributes: environment, price, service. The weighted blend predicts whether customers revisit or share on social media. My customerSatisfactionScore isn't yet decomposed this way, but this paper justifies my plan to report satisfaction as a multi-dimensional vector rather than a single scalar. The scripted scene drops the service attribute and shows the overall face decay from 😊 to 😐 to 😟.

ch3a.title: Hirsch 2025 · Ethogram: 9 cat states
ch3a.narrative: Hirsch et al. (2025) compressed 227 hours of observation into a 9-state ethogram running from "hidden" and "resting" all the way to "exploring" and "playing," each with a measured frequency. What surprised me most was PLAY at just 0.3% and OUT_OF_LOUNGE (back in the cat room) at 31.6%. That directly drives my cat FSM's probability table and explains why the cat room often looks empty in playback: that 31.6% is now a state, not a sprite on screen. The wheel on the right draws the 9 sectors proportional to Hirsch's measurements; one full rotation of the pointer = one complete observation sample.

ch3b.title: Hirsch 2025 · Vertical stratification: cats flee upward
ch3b.narrative: Hirsch's Figure 4b captures a simple but important finding: as customer count rises, cats migrate from floor to furniture to shelf. That maps onto my simulator's verticalLevel attribute and the rule that shelf preference scales with occupancy. The animation shows occupancy ramping 20% → 90% and the cat hopping floor → furniture → shelf in response.

ch3c.title: Hirsch 2025 · Interaction matrix: 4 customer types × 4 modes
ch3c.narrative: Hirsch's Figure 6 reports differences across customer type (woman / man / girl / boy) × interaction mode (no interaction / passing / looking / touching). Women had the highest contact rate; boys drove the most active-avoidance. This seeded my simulator's customer-profile system, where each type carries its own interaction-probability vector. The animation walks one woman's interaction over time: seated (no interaction) → noticing the cat (non-contact) → actually petting (contact).

ch4.title: Animal welfare: customers aren't the only stakeholders
ch4.narrative: Ropski et al. (2023) reminded me that cats are stakeholders too, not just the service surface. Combined with Hirsch 2025's discussion of welfare sub-indicators (play / explore / maintenance = higher is better; hiding / alert = lower is better), I built a 0–5 CatWelfareScore and plotted it as a Pareto frontier against customer satisfaction. The animation contrasts a low-occupancy and high-occupancy scenario side by side so "a packed café is a cat disaster" becomes obvious at a glance.

ch5.title: Pulling it back into NekoServe
ch5.narrative: The finale threads every paper into the complete customer journey pipeline: Poisson arrival → possible balking or successful seating → ordering and dining → waiting for cat interaction → exit or reneging → comparing results against the Hirsch benchmark. Every stage is backed by at least one paper. Click any node in the flow below to jump straight to the matching page in the simulator and watch the logic run live.

pipeline.title: NekoServe simulation pipeline × citations
pipeline.caption: Arrows show flow direction. "ref" count under each node indicates how many papers back that stage.

popover.jumpLabel: See this stage run live in the simulator

stageSummary.arrive: Customers arrive as a Poisson process. Each arrival is independent, with an average of one per customerArrivalInterval minutes; actual gaps are drawn from Exp(λ).
stageSummary.queue: When seats are full, customers queue. Ancker & Gafarian (1963) supplies the classical balking (turn around on sight of the queue) and reneging (abandon after waiting) model; Little (1961) gives the expected queue length.
stageSummary.seat: When a seat frees up, it's assigned to the queue head. Little's Law ties seat utilization to average queue length.
stageSummary.serve: Order, wait for the kitchen, then dine. Service times are drawn from an exponential distribution with rate μ (staff's output-per-unit-time).
stageSummary.cat: Once seated, a customer may be visited by a cat. Hirsch 2025's 9-state ethogram decides each cat's current activity; the vertical-level and interaction matrix decide who gets visited and how.
stageSummary.exit: Two exit paths: a satisfied customer who finished their meal (normal exit) or one who ran out of patience (reneging, per Dbeis & Al-Sahili 2024). Li 2025's three-attribute satisfaction also influences return intent here.
stageSummary.verify: After the simulation run, we compare the cat behavior distribution to Hirsch 2025's field measurements and compute a validation score. Ropski 2023's welfare lens reinforces the cat-rights check at this stage.

appendix.title: 📑 Appendix: full reference list
appendix.intro: APA format, BibTeX entries, and reverse parameter index for every cited work. Copy individual entries or grab the whole .bib below.

symbols.meaning: Time-average number of customers in the system (both queued and in-service)
Arrival rate (customers per unit time), commonly in customers/min
Average time a customer spends in the system (queue + service)
Plain multiplication: three quantities multiply exactly, independent of any distributional assumption
Dbeis 2024 corrected utilization, netted out of fake busyness from reneging
Nominal arrival rate (customers walking in the door per unit time)
Reneging rate: customers giving up per unit time
Service rate: customers one server can finish per unit time
